cards.title: The Constitution of India
cards.subtitle: The Republic's Foundation
cards.description: Adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26 November 1949 and brought into force on 26 January 1950, it is the supreme law of India and the longest written constitution of any sovereign nation. It establishes India as a sovereign, socialist, secular, democratic republic.

cards.title: The Constituent Assembly
cards.subtitle: Framers of the Republic
cards.description: Constituted in 1946, the Assembly drafted the Constitution of India over nearly three years, holding its first sitting on 9 December 1946. Dr. Rajendra Prasad served as its President and Dr. B.R. Ambedkar chaired its Drafting Committee.

cards.title: B.R. Ambedkar
cards.subtitle: Chairman of the Drafting Committee
cards.description: Dr. Bhimrao Ramji Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Constituent Assembly and is widely regarded as the principal architect of the Indian Constitution. He also served as independent India's first Law Minister.

cards.title: The Preamble
cards.subtitle: The Constitution's Soul
cards.description: The introductory statement of the Constitution declares India to be a sovereign, socialist, secular, democratic republic securing justice, liberty, equality and fraternity to its citizens. The words 'socialist' and 'secular' were added by the 42nd Amendment in 1976.

cards.title: Fundamental Rights
cards.subtitle: Guarantees to the Citizen
cards.description: Enshrined in Part III of the Constitution, these rights include equality, freedom, protection against exploitation, freedom of religion, cultural and educational rights, and the right to constitutional remedies. They are enforceable by the courts.

cards.title: Directive Principles of State Policy
cards.subtitle: Goals of Governance
cards.description: Contained in Part IV of the Constitution, these are guidelines for the State to frame laws and policies promoting social and economic welfare. Though not enforceable by courts, they are fundamental in the governance of the country.

cards.title: Fundamental Duties
cards.subtitle: Obligations of the Citizen
cards.description: Added by the 42nd Amendment in 1976 as Part IV-A, the Fundamental Duties list moral obligations of citizens, such as respecting the Constitution, the national flag and anthem, and protecting the environment. The list now contains eleven duties.

cards.title: Republic Day
cards.subtitle: Birth of the Republic
cards.description: On 26 January 1950 the Constitution of India came into force, transforming the nation into a republic with an elected head of state. The date was chosen to honour the 1930 Purna Swaraj declaration and is celebrated annually with a ceremonial parade in New Delhi.

cards.title: The National Emblem
cards.subtitle: Lions of Sarnath
cards.description: Adapted from the Lion Capital of Ashoka at Sarnath, the State Emblem of India was adopted on 26 January 1950. It bears the motto 'Satyameva Jayate' (Truth Alone Triumphs) inscribed below in Devanagari script.

cards.title: Universal Adult Franchise
cards.subtitle: One Citizen, One Vote
cards.description: The Constitution granted every adult citizen the right to vote regardless of caste, religion, gender, wealth or education. The voting age was lowered from 21 to 18 years by the 61st Amendment in 1988.

cards.title: Federalism
cards.subtitle: Union and States
cards.description: India follows a federal structure with a division of powers between the Union and the States, while retaining strong unitary features. The Constitution describes India as a 'Union of States.'

cards.title: The Union, State and Concurrent Lists
cards.subtitle: Division of Powers
cards.description: The Seventh Schedule divides legislative powers between the Union and the States through three lists. The Union List covers national subjects, the State List local ones, and the Concurrent List subjects on which both may legislate.

cards.title: Judicial Review
cards.subtitle: Courts as Guardians
cards.description: Judicial review is the power of the courts to examine the constitutional validity of laws and executive actions. The Supreme Court and High Courts can strike down legislation that violates the Constitution.

cards.title: The Amendment Process
cards.subtitle: Changing the Constitution
cards.description: Article 368 lays down the procedure for amending the Constitution, requiring a special majority in Parliament and, for certain provisions, ratification by the states. The Constitution has been amended over a hundred times since 1950.

cards.title: Citizenship
cards.subtitle: Membership of the Nation
cards.description: Part II of the Constitution and the Citizenship Act of 1955 define who is a citizen of India and how citizenship may be acquired or lost. India provides for a single, unified citizenship for the whole country.

cards.title: Parliament of India
cards.subtitle: The Supreme Legislature
cards.description: The Parliament of India is the supreme legislative body, consisting of the President and two Houses, the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha. It makes laws for the country and oversees the executive.

cards.title: The Lok Sabha
cards.subtitle: House of the People
cards.description: The Lok Sabha, or House of the People, is the lower house of Parliament whose members are directly elected by the people. It has a maximum strength of 552 members and a normal term of five years.

cards.title: The Rajya Sabha
cards.subtitle: Council of States
cards.description: The Rajya Sabha, or Council of States, is the upper house of Parliament representing the states and union territories. It is a permanent body that is not subject to dissolution, with a third of its members retiring every two years.

cards.title: The President of India
cards.subtitle: Head of State
cards.description: The President is the constitutional head of state and the first citizen of India, elected by an electoral college of members of Parliament and state legislatures. The President is also the supreme commander of the armed forces.

cards.title: The Vice-President of India
cards.subtitle: Chairman of the Rajya Sabha
cards.description: The Vice-President is the second-highest constitutional office in India and serves as the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha. The Vice-President acts as President during a vacancy in that office.

cards.title: The Prime Minister
cards.subtitle: Head of Government
cards.description: The Prime Minister is the head of the Union government and leader of the Council of Ministers, appointed by the President and usually the leader of the majority in the Lok Sabha. The office holds the real executive authority of the Union.

cards.title: The Council of Ministers
cards.subtitle: The Union Cabinet
cards.description: The Council of Ministers, headed by the Prime Minister, aids and advises the President in the exercise of executive functions. It is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha.

cards.title: The Supreme Court of India
cards.subtitle: Apex of Justice
cards.description: Established on 28 January 1950, the Supreme Court is the highest judicial body and the final court of appeal in India. It is the guardian of the Constitution and exercises original, appellate and advisory jurisdiction.

cards.title: The High Courts
cards.subtitle: Pillars of State Justice
cards.description: High Courts are the highest courts of justice within each state or group of states, exercising jurisdiction over subordinate courts. The Calcutta High Court, established in 1862, is the oldest in India.

cards.title: The Election Commission of India
cards.subtitle: Custodian of Elections
cards.description: Established on 25 January 1950, the Election Commission is an autonomous constitutional body that administers elections to Parliament, state legislatures, and the offices of President and Vice-President. It is headed by the Chief Election Commissioner.

cards.title: The Electronic Voting Machine
cards.subtitle: Instrument of the Ballot
cards.description: Electronic Voting Machines are used to record votes in Indian elections, replacing paper ballots. First used in a 1982 by-election in Kerala, they were adopted nationwide for general elections by 2004 and are paired with VVPAT units.

cards.title: The Comptroller and Auditor General
cards.subtitle: Auditor of the Nation
cards.description: The Comptroller and Auditor General of India is a constitutional authority that audits the accounts of the Union and state governments. Dr. Ambedkar described the office as one of the most important under the Constitution.

cards.title: The Union Public Service Commission
cards.subtitle: Gateway to the Services
cards.description: The UPSC is the central agency that conducts examinations for recruitment to the All India Services and central civil services. It is a constitutional body established under Article 315.

cards.title: The Attorney General of India
cards.subtitle: First Law Officer
cards.description: The Attorney General is the highest law officer of the Union and the chief legal adviser to the Government of India. M.C. Setalvad served as the first Attorney General of independent India.

cards.title: The Reserve Bank of India
cards.subtitle: Guardian of the Rupee
cards.description: Established in 1935 and nationalised in 1949, the Reserve Bank of India is the country's central bank, responsible for monetary policy and issuing currency. It regulates the banking and financial system of the nation.

cards.title: The Planning Commission
cards.subtitle: Architect of Five-Year Plans
cards.description: Set up in 1950, the Planning Commission formulated India's Five-Year Plans to guide economic and social development. It functioned until 2014, when it was replaced by NITI Aayog.

cards.title: NITI Aayog
cards.subtitle: The Policy Think Tank
cards.description: The National Institution for Transforming India was established in 2015 as the successor to the Planning Commission. It serves as the government's premier policy think tank, fostering cooperative federalism.

cards.title: The Finance Commission
cards.subtitle: Sharer of Revenues
cards.description: A constitutional body formed every five years under Article 280, the Finance Commission recommends how tax revenues are to be shared between the Union and the states. The first Finance Commission was constituted in 1951.

cards.title: Panchayati Raj
cards.subtitle: Democracy at the Grassroots
cards.description: The 73rd Constitutional Amendment of 1992 gave constitutional status to rural local self-government through a three-tier panchayat system. It mandated regular elections and reservation of seats for women and weaker sections.

cards.title: Urban Local Bodies
cards.subtitle: Self-Government in Cities
cards.description: The 74th Constitutional Amendment of 1992 gave constitutional status to urban local bodies such as municipalities and municipal corporations. It established a framework for self-government in towns and cities.

cards.title: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha
cards.subtitle: Master of the House
cards.description: The Speaker is the presiding officer of the Lok Sabha, responsible for conducting its business and maintaining order. G.V. Mavalankar was the first Speaker of independent India's Lok Sabha.

cards.title: State Governors
cards.subtitle: Heads of the States
cards.description: The Governor is the constitutional head of a state, appointed by the President, and acts on the advice of the state Council of Ministers. The Governor performs a role at the state level analogous to that of the President at the Union level.

cards.title: State Legislative Assemblies
cards.subtitle: Voice of the States
cards.description: The Vidhan Sabha, or Legislative Assembly, is the directly elected lawmaking body of a state, with members chosen by the people of that state. Some states also have a Legislative Council as an upper house.

cards.title: Parliament House
cards.subtitle: Seat of the Legislature
cards.description: The Parliament House in New Delhi, designed by Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker, was inaugurated in 1927 and housed India's legislature for decades. A new Parliament building was inaugurated in 2023.

cards.title: Rashtrapati Bhavan
cards.subtitle: Home of the President
cards.description: Rashtrapati Bhavan in New Delhi is the official residence of the President of India, designed by Edwin Lutyens and completed in 1929. It was originally built as the residence of the British Viceroy.

cards.title: The Lok Sabha Mace
cards.subtitle: Symbol of Authority
cards.description: The ceremonial mace, known as the Sengol in the new Parliament, symbolises the authority of the presiding officer and the House. It is carried in and placed near the Speaker's chair when the House is in session.

cards.title: Jawaharlal Nehru
cards.subtitle: The First Prime Minister
cards.description: Jawaharlal Nehru served as the first Prime Minister of India from 1947 until his death in 1964, the longest-serving holder of the office. He delivered the 'Tryst with Destiny' speech on the eve of independence.

cards.title: Lal Bahadur Shastri
cards.subtitle: The Second Prime Minister
cards.description: Lal Bahadur Shastri served as Prime Minister from 1964 to 1966 and led the country during the 1965 war. He coined the slogan 'Jai Jawan Jai Kisan' and died in Tashkent in 1966.

cards.title: Indira Gandhi
cards.subtitle: First Woman Prime Minister
cards.description: Indira Gandhi was the first and, to date, only woman to serve as Prime Minister of India, holding office from 1966 to 1977 and again from 1980 to 1984. She was the daughter of Jawaharlal Nehru.

cards.title: Morarji Desai
cards.subtitle: First Non-Congress Prime Minister
cards.description: Morarji Desai became Prime Minister in 1977 as the head of the Janata Party government, the first Prime Minister not from the Indian National Congress. He is also the only Indian to have received Pakistan's highest civilian honour.

cards.title: Charan Singh
cards.subtitle: Champion of the Farmer
cards.description: Charan Singh served as Prime Minister from July 1979 to January 1980 and was known for his focus on rural and agrarian issues. His birthday, 23 December, is observed as Kisan Diwas.

cards.title: Rajiv Gandhi
cards.subtitle: Youngest Prime Minister
cards.description: Rajiv Gandhi became the youngest Prime Minister of India at age 40 in 1984, serving until 1989. He is associated with initiatives to modernise telecommunications and computing in India.

cards.title: V.P. Singh
cards.subtitle: Prime Minister of Mandal
cards.description: Vishwanath Pratap Singh served as Prime Minister from 1989 to 1990 at the head of the National Front coalition. His government implemented the recommendations of the Mandal Commission on reservations.

cards.title: Chandra Shekhar
cards.subtitle: The Young Turk
cards.description: Chandra Shekhar served as Prime Minister from November 1990 to June 1991, leading a breakaway faction with outside support. He was known for his 1983 Bharat Yatra, a long march across the country on foot.

cards.title: P.V. Narasimha Rao
cards.subtitle: Architect of Reform
cards.description: P.V. Narasimha Rao was Prime Minister from 1991 to 1996 and presided over the economic liberalisation of India. He was the first Prime Minister from South India to serve a full term.

cards.title: H.D. Deve Gowda
cards.subtitle: Son of the Soil
cards.description: H.D. Deve Gowda served as Prime Minister from 1996 to 1997 at the head of the United Front coalition. He had earlier served as Chief Minister of Karnataka.

cards.title: I.K. Gujral
cards.subtitle: The Diplomat Premier
cards.description: Inder Kumar Gujral served as Prime Minister from 1997 to 1998 and was a career diplomat and parliamentarian. He is associated with the 'Gujral Doctrine' on relations with neighbouring countries.

cards.title: Atal Bihari Vajpayee
cards.subtitle: The Orator Statesman
cards.description: Atal Bihari Vajpayee served three terms as Prime Minister, including a full term from 1999 to 2004, and was the first non-Congress Prime Minister to do so. His birthday, 25 December, is observed as Good Governance Day.

cards.title: Manmohan Singh
cards.subtitle: The Economist Premier
cards.description: Manmohan Singh served as Prime Minister from 2004 to 2014 and was earlier the Finance Minister who led the 1991 economic reforms. He was the first Sikh to hold the office of Prime Minister.

cards.title: Narendra Modi
cards.subtitle: Prime Minister Since 2014
cards.description: Narendra Modi became Prime Minister of India in 2014 and was re-elected in 2019 and 2024. He previously served as Chief Minister of Gujarat for over a decade.

cards.title: Rajendra Prasad
cards.subtitle: The First President
cards.description: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India, serving from 1950 to 1962, the longest tenure in the office. He had earlier presided over the Constituent Assembly.

cards.title: S. Radhakrishnan
cards.subtitle: The Philosopher President
cards.description: Dr. Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan was a renowned philosopher who served as the first Vice-President and the second President of India. His birthday, 5 September, is celebrated as Teachers' Day.

cards.title: Zakir Husain
cards.subtitle: The Educator President
cards.description: Dr. Zakir Husain was the third President of India and the first Muslim to hold the office, serving from 1967 until his death in 1969. He was also an eminent educationist associated with Jamia Millia Islamia.

cards.title: V.V. Giri
cards.subtitle: The Labour Leader President
cards.description: Varahagiri Venkata Giri served as the fourth President of India from 1969 to 1974, having earlier been a trade union leader. He was the only person to be elected President as an independent candidate.

cards.title: Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed
cards.subtitle: The Fifth President
cards.description: Fakhruddin Ali Ahmed was the fifth President of India, serving from 1974 until his death in 1977. He was the second Muslim to hold the office of President.

cards.title: Neelam Sanjiva Reddy
cards.subtitle: From Speaker to President
cards.description: Neelam Sanjiva Reddy was the sixth President of India, serving from 1977 to 1982, and the youngest to assume the office. He had earlier been Speaker of the Lok Sabha and Chief Minister of Andhra Pradesh.

cards.title: Giani Zail Singh
cards.subtitle: The Seventh President
cards.description: Giani Zail Singh was the seventh President of India, serving from 1982 to 1987, and the first Sikh to hold the office. He had earlier served as Chief Minister of Punjab and as Union Home Minister.

cards.title: R. Venkataraman
cards.subtitle: The Eighth President
cards.description: Ramaswamy Venkataraman was the eighth President of India, serving from 1987 to 1992. During his tenure he appointed several Prime Ministers in an era of coalition politics.

cards.title: K.R. Narayanan
cards.subtitle: The Diplomat President
cards.description: Kocheril Raman Narayanan was the tenth President of India, serving from 1997 to 2002, and the first Dalit to hold the office. He had earlier been a distinguished diplomat and Vice-President.

cards.title: A.P.J. Abdul Kalam
cards.subtitle: The People's President
cards.description: Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam was the eleventh President of India, serving from 2002 to 2007, and a renowned aerospace scientist. He is widely remembered for his work in India's space and missile programmes.

cards.title: Pratibha Patil
cards.subtitle: First Woman President
cards.description: Pratibha Patil was the twelfth President of India, serving from 2007 to 2012, and the first woman to hold the office. She had earlier served as Governor of Rajasthan.

cards.title: Pranab Mukherjee
cards.subtitle: The Statesman President
cards.description: Pranab Mukherjee was the thirteenth President of India, serving from 2012 to 2017. Before becoming President he held several senior cabinet positions, including Finance Minister and External Affairs Minister.

cards.title: Droupadi Murmu
cards.subtitle: President Since 2022
cards.description: Droupadi Murmu became the fifteenth President of India in 2022, the first person from a tribal community and the second woman to hold the office. She had earlier served as Governor of Jharkhand.

cards.title: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel
cards.subtitle: The Iron Man of India
cards.description: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel was independent India's first Deputy Prime Minister and Home Minister, who led the integration of over 500 princely states into the Union. His birthday, 31 October, is observed as National Unity Day.

cards.title: Maulana Abul Kalam Azad
cards.subtitle: First Education Minister
cards.description: Maulana Abul Kalam Azad was independent India's first Minister of Education, serving from 1947 to 1958. His birthday, 11 November, is celebrated as National Education Day.

cards.title: G.V. Mavalankar
cards.subtitle: The First Speaker
cards.description: Ganesh Vasudev Mavalankar was the first Speaker of the Lok Sabha, serving from 1952 to 1956. He is often called the 'Father of the Lok Sabha' for shaping its early procedures.

cards.title: Sukumar Sen
cards.subtitle: First Chief Election Commissioner
cards.description: Sukumar Sen was the first Chief Election Commissioner of India, who organised the first two general elections of 1951-52 and 1957. He oversaw the enormous logistical task of conducting elections for a newly independent nation.

cards.title: T.N. Seshan
cards.subtitle: Reformer of Elections
cards.description: T.N. Seshan served as Chief Election Commissioner from 1990 to 1996 and is known for vigorously enforcing the model code of conduct. His tenure strengthened the autonomy and authority of the Election Commission.

cards.title: Jayaprakash Narayan
cards.subtitle: Leader of Total Revolution
cards.description: Jayaprakash Narayan, known as JP, led a mass movement in the mid-1970s calling for 'Total Revolution.' His movement became a focal point of opposition politics during that period.

cards.title: Ram Manohar Lohia
cards.subtitle: The Socialist Thinker
cards.description: Ram Manohar Lohia was a socialist leader, thinker and parliamentarian who shaped opposition politics in independent India. He advocated decentralisation and was a prominent voice in the Lok Sabha.

cards.title: Jagjivan Ram
cards.subtitle: Babuji of Indian Politics
cards.description: Jagjivan Ram was a veteran parliamentarian who held several cabinet portfolios over decades, including Defence, and later served as Deputy Prime Minister. He was the longest-serving member of the Indian Cabinet.

cards.title: V.K. Krishna Menon
cards.subtitle: Voice at the United Nations
cards.description: V.K. Krishna Menon was a diplomat and statesman who served as Defence Minister and as India's representative at the United Nations. He is remembered for a marathon speech on Kashmir at the UN Security Council in 1957.

cards.title: C.D. Deshmukh
cards.subtitle: The Economist Statesman
cards.description: Chintaman Dwarakanath Deshmukh was the first Indian Governor of the Reserve Bank of India and later served as Union Finance Minister. He played a key role in shaping India's early economic institutions.

cards.title: Kanshi Ram
cards.subtitle: Voice of Social Justice
cards.description: Kanshi Ram was a political organiser who founded movements and a party to mobilise marginalised communities in Indian politics. He is recognised for his focus on social justice and political empowerment.

cards.title: B.N. Rau
cards.subtitle: Constitutional Adviser
cards.description: Sir Benegal Narsing Rau was the Constitutional Adviser to the Constituent Assembly, who prepared the initial draft of the Constitution. He later served as a judge of the International Court of Justice.

cards.title: Sucheta Kripalani
cards.subtitle: First Woman Chief Minister
cards.description: Sucheta Kripalani was the first woman to become a Chief Minister of an Indian state, heading the government of Uttar Pradesh from 1963 to 1967. She was an active participant in the Constituent Assembly.

cards.title: The First General Election
cards.subtitle: The Great Experiment
cards.description: Held from October 1951 to February 1952, India's first general election was the largest democratic exercise in the world at that time. Over 170 million voters were eligible, most of them voting for the first time.

cards.title: The States Reorganisation Act
cards.subtitle: Redrawing the Map
cards.description: Enacted in 1956, the States Reorganisation Act reorganised the boundaries of India's states largely along linguistic lines. It was based on the recommendations of the States Reorganisation Commission.

cards.title: The Kesavananda Bharati Case
cards.subtitle: The Basic Structure Doctrine
cards.description: In this 1973 case, the Supreme Court held that Parliament cannot amend the 'basic structure' of the Constitution. It was decided by the largest constitutional bench in Indian history.

cards.title: The 42nd Amendment
cards.subtitle: The Mini-Constitution
cards.description: Passed in 1976, the 42nd Amendment made wide-ranging changes to the Constitution, including adding the words 'socialist' and 'secular' to the Preamble and introducing Fundamental Duties. It is often called the 'Mini-Constitution.'

cards.title: The Emergency
cards.subtitle: A Constitutional Crisis
cards.description: A national Emergency was declared on 25 June 1975 and remained in force until 21 March 1977, during which civil liberties were curtailed. It was the first and only Emergency proclaimed on grounds of internal disturbance.

cards.title: The Janata Party Government
cards.subtitle: The First Change of Power
cards.description: Formed after the 1977 general election, the Janata Party government was the first at the Union level not led by the Congress. It marked the first peaceful transfer of power to an opposition coalition.

cards.title: The Anti-Defection Law
cards.subtitle: Guarding Party Loyalty
cards.description: Introduced by the 52nd Amendment in 1985, the Anti-Defection Law adds the Tenth Schedule to disqualify legislators who switch parties. It aims to curb political defections and bring stability to governments.

cards.title: The Mandal Commission
cards.subtitle: The Question of Reservation
cards.description: Set up in 1979 and reporting in 1980, the Mandal Commission examined the conditions of socially and educationally backward classes. Its recommendation of reservations in central government jobs was implemented in 1990.

cards.title: Economic Liberalisation
cards.subtitle: The Reforms of 1991
cards.description: In 1991, India undertook major economic reforms that liberalised trade, reduced state controls, and opened the economy to private and foreign investment. The reforms were introduced amid a balance-of-payments crisis.

cards.title: The Right to Information Act
cards.subtitle: Power to the Citizen
cards.description: Enacted in 2005, the Right to Information Act empowers citizens to seek information from public authorities. It aims to promote transparency and accountability in the working of the government.

cards.title: MGNREGA
cards.subtitle: The Right to Work
cards.description: The Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act, enacted in 2005, guarantees a hundred days of wage employment in a year to rural households. It is one of the largest public employment programmes in the world.

cards.title: The Goods and Services Tax
cards.subtitle: One Nation, One Tax
cards.description: Introduced on 1 July 2017 through the 101st Constitutional Amendment, the Goods and Services Tax unified many indirect taxes into a single system. It is administered with the help of the GST Council.

cards.title: The Creation of Telangana
cards.subtitle: India's Newest State
cards.description: Telangana was formed on 2 June 2014 as the 29th state of India, carved out of Andhra Pradesh. It became the most recent state created through the reorganisation of an existing one.

cards.title: The Era of Coalitions
cards.subtitle: Politics of Alliance
cards.description: From the late 1980s, national politics entered an era in which no single party held a Lok Sabha majority, leading to coalition governments. Alliances such as the National Front, United Front, NDA and UPA emerged in this period.

cards.title: Delimitation
cards.subtitle: Drawing the Constituencies
cards.description: Delimitation is the process of redrawing the boundaries of electoral constituencies to reflect changes in population. It is carried out by an independent Delimitation Commission established by an Act of Parliament.

cards.title: Secularism
cards.subtitle: A Constitutional Principle
cards.description: As a constitutional principle, secularism in India means the state treats all religions equally and has no official religion. The word 'secular' was added to the Preamble by the 42nd Amendment in 1976.

cards.title: Separation of Powers
cards.subtitle: The Three Branches
cards.description: Separation of powers is the principle that the legislature, executive and judiciary function in their own spheres with checks and balances. It is regarded as part of the basic structure of the Constitution.

cards.title: The No-Confidence Motion
cards.subtitle: Test of the Majority
cards.description: A no-confidence motion is moved in the Lok Sabha to test whether the Council of Ministers still commands the confidence of the House. If passed, the government is required to resign.

cards.title: The Rule of Law
cards.subtitle: Equality Before the Law
cards.description: The rule of law holds that all persons and authorities are subject to and accountable under the law, which is applied equally to all. It is a foundational principle of the Indian constitutional order and forms part of its basic structure.
